curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is chemistry?; Scientific questions, evidence, and explanations; The scientific method; Safety in the laboratory
curriculum.lesson_notes: Introduce chemistry as the study of matter. The scientific method: ask a question, suggest an explanation, collect evidence, check if evidence supports explanation. Key lab safety rules: goggles, no tasting, handle glassware carefully. Use the textbook Rabia boiling water story as a worked example.
curriculum.activity: Lab Safety Scavenger Hunt: Students identify 10 safety items in the lab and write the purpose of each.
curriculum.homework: Write a short paragraph: What question would you like chemistry to answer and how might you investigate it? (5-8 sentences)

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Solids, liquids, and gases; The particle theory of matter; Particle arrangement, movement, and spacing
curriculum.lesson_notes: All matter is made of tiny particles. Solids: closely packed, fixed positions, vibrate. Liquids: close but move past each other. Gases: far apart, rapid random movement. Fixed shape and volume (solid); fixed volume any shape (liquid); fills any container (gas).
curriculum.activity: Student Particle Dance: Students become particles and arrange themselves as solid, liquid, or gas on teacher command.
curriculum.homework: Draw and label the particle arrangement of a solid, liquid, and gas. List two observable properties of each state.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Melting, freezing; Boiling, evaporating, condensing; Sublimation; Energy and changes of state
curriculum.lesson_notes: Physical changes: same substance, particles rearranged. Melting (solid-liquid), freezing (liquid-solid), boiling (liquid-gas throughout), evaporation (liquid-gas at surface), condensation (gas-liquid), sublimation (solid-gas). Energy needed for melting/boiling; released on freezing/condensation.
curriculum.activity: Ice Cube Race: Groups given identical ice cubes in different conditions (open air, cotton wool, near heat). Observe and record which melts fastest. Explain using particle theory.
curriculum.homework: Complete a flow diagram: Solid to Liquid to Gas and reverse, naming each change. Answer: Why does a puddle disappear on a sunny day?

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is dissolving?; Solute, solvent, solution; Particle theory of dissolving; Factors affecting dissolving
curriculum.lesson_notes: Dissolving: solute particles separate and fill spaces between solvent particles forming a clear solution. Solute is NOT destroyed — it can be recovered. Factors affecting dissolving speed: temperature (higher = faster), stirring, particle size of solute (smaller = faster).
curriculum.activity: Dissolving Investigation: Dissolve sugar in cold unstirred water, cold stirred water, and warm stirred water. Time each. Record in table and draw conclusions.
curriculum.homework: A student adds salt to water and stirs — the salt disappears. Write 3 sentences explaining to a friend why the salt is NOT gone forever.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is an element?; Pure substances vs mixtures; Introduction to the Periodic Table; Metals vs non-metals overview
curriculum.lesson_notes: Elements: pure substances made of one type of atom. ~118 elements. Periodic Table lists elements by atomic number. Metals (left/centre) and non-metals (right), separated by a stepped line. Key symbols to learn: H, O, C, N, Fe, Na, Ca, Cl, Au, Ag.
curriculum.activity: Element Symbol Snap: Card matching game — element names and symbols. Students play in pairs or as a class quiz.
curriculum.homework: Write names and symbols of 10 common elements from memory. Draw a simple Periodic Table outline and shade metals and non-metals with different colours.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Physical properties of metals; Malleability, ductility, conductivity; Sonority and density; Uses linked to properties
curriculum.lesson_notes: Metals: shiny when cut, high melting/boiling points, good conductors of heat and electricity, malleable, ductile, sonorous, high density. Exceptions: mercury is liquid at room temp; sodium is very soft. Copper (ductile, conductor), gold (shiny, malleable), iron (strong) — link properties to uses.
curriculum.activity: Metal Properties Observation: Examine iron, copper, and aluminium. Test bending, weight, and electrical conductivity. Record in a table.
curriculum.homework: Choose two metals. Write a paragraph explaining three properties of each and one use that relies on those properties.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Physical properties of non-metals; Solid non-metals; Gaseous non-metals; Metalloids; Carbon allotropes: diamond and graphite
curriculum.lesson_notes: Non-metals: low melting/boiling points, poor conductors, brittle when solid. Many are gases at room temp. Carbon allotropes: diamond (very hard, poor conductor), graphite (soft, good conductor — exception among non-metals). Silicon = metalloid/semiconductor.
curriculum.activity: Venn Diagram Activity: Place property cards in Venn diagram sections: Metals Only / Both / Non-Metals Only.
curriculum.homework: Compare diamond and graphite in a table: Structure, Hardness, Electrical Conductivity, Uses.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Hardness, density, flexibility, strength; Linking properties to uses; Polymers introduction; Metal alloys introduction
curriculum.lesson_notes: Materials chosen because their properties match what is needed. Key properties: hardness, density, flexibility, strength, thermal conductivity. Polymers (plastics): lightweight, flexible, poor conductors. Alloys: steel (iron+carbon) stronger than iron; brass (copper+zinc) harder than copper.
curriculum.activity: Materials Sorting Game: Classify everyday objects (plastic bag, metal spoon, rubber band, wooden stick, fabric) by properties and explain why each material was chosen.
curriculum.homework: For a saucepan, bicycle frame, and raincoat: identify the key material, list two important properties, explain why those properties make it suitable.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Variables (independent, dependent, control); Designing a fair test; Collecting and recording data; Tables and graphs
curriculum.lesson_notes: Fair test: change only ONE variable (independent), measure one outcome (dependent), keep all others the same (control). Record data in tables with headings and units. Bar charts for categories; line graphs for continuous data. Conclusions must be based on evidence.
curriculum.activity: Paper Plane Investigation: Test how the number of folds in a wing affects distance flown. Identify variables, plan fair test, collect data, plot results.
curriculum.homework: A student tests whether sunlight affects dissolving time. Identify: independent variable, dependent variable, two control variables, and how to record results.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Term 1 Review — States of Matter and Materials
curriculum.subtopics: Particle theory; Changes of state; Dissolving; Elements, metals, non-metals; Scientific enquiry skills
curriculum.lesson_notes: Consolidation of all Term 1 content. Review particle theory, states of matter, changes of state, dissolving, elements and Periodic Table, properties of metals and non-metals, and designing fair tests. Identify gaps through questioning.
curriculum.activity: Team Quiz: Four teams, 20 questions covering all Term 1 topics. Teams discuss before answering. Award points and review each answer.
curriculum.homework: Revise all Term 1 topics. Write a one-page summary of the most important ideas from Term 1 in your own words.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What are acids and alkalis?; Everyday examples; Indicators; Hazard symbols
curriculum.lesson_notes: Acids: vinegar, lemon juice, HCl. Alkalis: soap, bleach, NaOH. Indicators change colour: litmus red in acid, blue in alkali. Hazard symbols for corrosive and irritant substances. Strong acids and alkalis are dangerous; many everyday acids (citric) are safe.
curriculum.activity: Indicator Rainbow: Red cabbage juice indicator tested with household substances. Record colour changes.
curriculum.homework: List five everyday acids and five everyday alkalis. State whether each is safe or dangerous and explain why.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: pH scale 0-14; Strong and weak acids/alkalis; Universal indicator; pH meters
curriculum.lesson_notes: pH less than 7 = acidic, pH 7 = neutral, pH greater than 7 = alkaline. Universal indicator gives range of colours. Key pH values: stomach acid ~2, vinegar ~3, water 7, blood 7.4, bleach 13. A change of 1 pH unit = tenfold change in strength.
curriculum.activity: pH Testing Station: Universal indicator added to various solutions. Observe colour and match to pH chart.
curriculum.homework: Draw and colour a pH scale 0-14. Mark: stomach acid, orange juice, water, blood, soapy water, oven cleaner. Classify each.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Acid + alkali = salt + water; Word equations; Real-world uses; Antacids
curriculum.lesson_notes: Neutralisation: acid + alkali produce salt and water at pH 7. Example: HCl + NaOH to NaCl + H2O. Uses: antacids neutralise stomach acid; lime neutralises acidic soil; toothpaste neutralises mouth acid.
curriculum.activity: Neutralisation Titration: Add dilute acid drop by drop to alkali with universal indicator. Observe colour change at neutral point.
curriculum.homework: Write word equations for: (a) nitric acid + potassium hydroxide, (b) sulfuric acid + calcium hydroxide. Name the salt produced in each. Explain why farmers add lime to acidic soil.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Differences; Evidence for chemical reactions; Irreversibility; Examples
curriculum.lesson_notes: Physical change: form altered, not chemical nature (melting, dissolving, cutting). Chemical change: new substance produced (burning, rusting, cooking). Evidence of chemical change: gas produced, colour change, energy change, precipitate forms, irreversibility.
curriculum.activity: Physical or Chemical Challenge: Classify 8 changes with justification (wax melting, iron rusting, paper burning, salt dissolving, etc.).
curriculum.homework: State physical or chemical and give one piece of evidence for: (a) wood burning, (b) water freezing, (c) copper turning green, (d) glass breaking.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Structure of the Earth; Three types of rocks; How rocks form
curriculum.lesson_notes: Earth structure: inner core (solid iron/nickel), outer core (liquid), mantle (semi-molten), crust (thin solid). Igneous: from cooling magma — granite (slow cooling, large crystals), basalt (fast cooling, small crystals). Sedimentary: compressed sediments. Metamorphic: heat and pressure.
curriculum.activity: Rock Collection Observation: Examine labelled rock samples or photos. Note colour, texture, grain size, fossils. Classify as igneous, sedimentary, or metamorphic.
curriculum.homework: Draw and annotate a diagram of the Earth's layers. Label and describe: inner core, outer core, mantle, crust.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Sedimentary rock formation; Limestone and chalk; Fossils; Age of the Earth
curriculum.lesson_notes: Sedimentary rocks form in layers over millions of years. Sediments settle in water, compressed and cemented. Limestone = CaCO3. Fossils: preserved remains or impressions in rock. Deeper layers = older. Earth is approximately 4.5 billion years old.
curriculum.activity: Fossil Impression Making: Press shell or leaf into plaster of Paris. Examine impression and discuss what it reveals about the original organism.
curriculum.homework: Explain how a fossil of a shell might be found in rock on a mountain. Include: how the shell became a fossil, how rock may have moved, and why this is evidence of geological change.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Weathering and erosion; Transport and deposition; Rock cycle diagram; Volcanoes
curriculum.lesson_notes: Rock cycle: continuous series transforming rocks. Weathering: physical (freeze-thaw) and chemical (acid rain). Erosion transports material. Deposition forms sediment. Sediment to sedimentary rock; heat and pressure to metamorphic; melting and cooling to igneous. Cycle has no beginning or end.
curriculum.activity: Rock Cycle Role Play: Students act as rock particles or process cards and physically trace a particle's journey through the rock cycle.
curriculum.homework: Draw and annotate the rock cycle. Include all three rock types and processes transforming one into another. Use labelled arrows.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is soil?; Components; Types of soil; Importance for farming
curriculum.lesson_notes: Soil: mineral particles + humus (decomposed organic matter) + water + air + living organisms. Clay: small particles, holds water, drains poorly. Sandy: large particles, drains quickly. Loam (ideal): mix of particle sizes. Soil pH affects crop growth. Lime corrects acidic soil.
curriculum.activity: Soil Investigation: Shake soil from two locations in water jars. Observe how particles settle. Test soil pH with universal indicator.
curriculum.homework: Explain the difference between clay and sandy soil. Why is loam soil best for most crops? Include particle size and water retention.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Enquiry Skills: Making Conclusions and Presenting Data
curriculum.subtopics: Reading tables and graphs; Drawing conclusions; Evaluating reliability; Repeating experiments
curriculum.lesson_notes: Read tables: identify patterns, note units, compare values. Read graphs: identify axes, spot trends and anomalies. Conclusions must be evidence-based. Reliability improves with repetition — calculate a mean. Anomalous results excluded from mean.
curriculum.activity: Data Detectives: Analyse a data table on rock dissolution in acid. Identify patterns, spot anomalies, calculate means, draw a bar chart, write a conclusion.
curriculum.homework: Given a data table: plot a bar chart, identify one anomalous result and explain why it might have occurred, and write a two-sentence conclusion.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Term 2 Review — Acids, Rocks, and Changes
curriculum.subtopics: Acids, alkalis, pH, neutralisation; Physical vs chemical changes; Rock types, rock cycle, fossils, soil
curriculum.lesson_notes: Consolidation of all Term 2 content. Connect ideas: acid rain causes chemical weathering of rocks; neutralisation is a chemical change; fossils in sedimentary rocks tell us the age of the Earth. Use concept mapping.
curriculum.activity: Concept Map Challenge: Create a concept map linking at least 15 key terms from Term 2 with explanatory phrases on arrows.
curriculum.homework: Complete 10 review questions from textbook Stage 7 Review. Check and correct answers with a different coloured pen.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Atoms as building blocks; Elements and symbols; Atomic number; Periodic Table overview
curriculum.lesson_notes: Atoms: smallest unit of an element. Every element = one kind of atom. Periodic Table lists elements by atomic number. Groups = vertical columns with similar properties. Periods = horizontal rows. Read element tiles: symbol, name, atomic number.
curriculum.activity: Periodic Table Treasure Hunt: Clue cards direct students to find element symbols using a reference Periodic Table.
curriculum.homework: Using a Periodic Table: (a) element with atomic number 8, (b) group sodium belongs to, (c) two elements in same period as carbon, (d) is chlorine a metal or non-metal.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Elements, compounds, mixtures; How compounds form; Common compounds; Chemical formulas introduction
curriculum.lesson_notes: Compound: two or more elements chemically joined in a fixed ratio. Cannot be separated physically. Chemical formulae: H2O, CO2, NaCl. Mixtures: components not chemically combined, keep own properties, can be physically separated. Air, salt water, and soil are mixtures.
curriculum.activity: Compound vs Mixture Sort: Sort substance name cards into compounds, elements, and mixtures with justification.
curriculum.homework: Explain the difference between a compound and a mixture. Give two examples of each. For each compound, write the chemical formula and state what elements it contains.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Naming binary compounds; Oxides, hydroxides, carbonates, sulfates; Writing and reading chemical formulae
curriculum.lesson_notes: Binary compounds end in -ide (sodium chloride, magnesium oxide). Three-element compounds with oxygen: -ate or -ite (sodium sulfate, calcium carbonate). Key formulae: MgO, CaCO3, H2SO4, NaOH. Subscript number shows number of atoms of that element.
curriculum.activity: Name-Formula Matching: Two card sets — compound names and formulas. Match pairs and state what elements each contains.
curriculum.homework: Name: MgO, KCl, CaCO3, Fe2O3. Write formulas for: sodium chloride, calcium oxide, iron sulfide. State whether each contains a metal and non-metal.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is a mixture?; Filtration; Decanting; When to use each technique
curriculum.lesson_notes: Mixture: substances not chemically combined; keep own properties; can be separated physically. Filtration: insoluble solid from liquid — residue on filter paper, filtrate passes through. Decanting: carefully pour liquid off settled solid. Used in water purification and industry.
curriculum.activity: Muddy Water Filtration: Filter sand-and-water through filter paper. Collect filtrate and examine residue. Compare clarity of original mixture and filtrate.
curriculum.homework: Draw and label a filtration apparatus. Explain residue and filtrate. State two situations where filtration is used in everyday life.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Evaporation; Distillation; Fractional distillation introduction
curriculum.lesson_notes: Evaporation: recover dissolved solid from solution. Simple distillation: recover pure liquid (solvent) from solution — heat, vapour rises, condenses, pure liquid collects. Fractional distillation: separate liquids with different boiling points — used for crude oil to produce petrol, diesel, kerosene.
curriculum.activity: Salt from Seawater: Evaporate saltwater in an evaporating dish over heat. Observe salt crystals forming. Connect to historical salt production from the sea.
curriculum.homework: Explain the difference between evaporation and distillation. When would you choose distillation over evaporation? Draw and label a simple distillation apparatus.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is chromatography?; Paper chromatography method; Rf values; Uses
curriculum.lesson_notes: Chromatography separates dissolved mixtures based on attraction to paper vs solvent. More attracted to solvent = travels further. Method: spot on pencil line, dip in solvent, allow to travel, dry, observe spots. Rf = distance by substance divided by distance by solvent front. Used in food testing, forensics, pharmaceutical analysis.
curriculum.activity: Ink Chromatography: Spot black water-based marker on filter paper strip. Dip in water. Observe colour separation. Measure Rf values for each component.
curriculum.homework: Solvent front moves 12 cm. Spots appear at 3, 8, and 12 cm. Calculate Rf for each spot. Which substance is most soluble in the solvent? Explain.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Stage 7 Review — Atoms, Compounds, and Separation
curriculum.subtopics: Atoms, elements, compounds; All separation techniques; Practice exam-style questions
curriculum.lesson_notes: Review: atoms and Periodic Table, elements vs compounds vs mixtures, naming and writing formulae, all four separation techniques and when to use each. Students should be able to explain why a particular technique is chosen for a given mixture.
curriculum.activity: Separation Technique Challenge: Cards with mixture scenarios. Groups choose the correct technique and present reasoning to the class.
curriculum.homework: Complete Stage 7 Review questions from the textbook. Revisit weak areas and write a revision summary.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Introducing Year 2 Concepts — States of Matter Revisited
curriculum.subtopics: Diffusion; Density and particle spacing; Gas pressure and particle collisions
curriculum.lesson_notes: Diffusion: gradual spreading of particles from high to low concentration without stirring. Affected by temperature. Density = mass divided by volume. Gases are less dense because particles are far apart. Gas pressure caused by gas particles colliding with container walls. Heating a gas increases particle speed and therefore pressure.
curriculum.activity: Diffusion Demonstration: Drop food colouring in cold and hot still water. Observe how quickly colour spreads. Discuss why the rate differs.
curriculum.homework: Explain: (a) what diffusion is and give one example; (b) why gases are less dense than solids; (c) why increasing temperature increases gas pressure.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: All Year 1 topics: states of matter, materials, acids/alkalis, rocks, elements, compounds, separation; Exam-style practice
curriculum.lesson_notes: Final synoptic review connecting all Year 1 content. Particle theory underpins states, dissolving, diffusion. Acids react with alkalis to form salts. Rocks are made of minerals (compounds). Apply knowledge to unfamiliar contexts — a key Cambridge skill.
curriculum.activity: Revision Flashcards: Students create 20 revision flashcards covering all Year 1 topics. Quiz each other in pairs.
curriculum.homework: Write a one-page Year 1 Chemistry Summary covering: particle theory, states of matter, elements/compounds/mixtures, acids and alkalis, rocks and rock cycle, and separation techniques. Use diagrams.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: End of Year Assessment and Feedback
curriculum.subtopics: Assessment covering all Year 1 content; Review; Target setting for Year 2
curriculum.lesson_notes: End-of-year assessment aligned with Cambridge Stage 7 objectives. Include: multiple choice, short written answers, data interpretation, and application questions. Return assessments with constructive feedback. Help students set targets for Year 2.
curriculum.activity: Assessment Day: Formal assessment under exam conditions (45-60 minutes). Span all terms and difficulty levels.
curriculum.homework: After receiving your paper: (a) two topics you answered well, (b) two topics with mistakes and what to improve, (c) one target for Year 2.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Protons, neutrons, electrons; Atomic number and mass number; Electron shells; Historical models
curriculum.lesson_notes: Sub-atomic particles: protons (positive, nucleus), neutrons (no charge, nucleus), electrons (negative, shells). Atomic number = number of protons. Mass number = protons + neutrons. Electron shells: 1st = 2, 2nd = 8, 3rd = 8. Historical models: Dalton, Thomson, Rutherford, Bohr. The atom is mostly empty space.
curriculum.activity: Build an Atom: Use coloured beads or clay balls to construct sodium (Z=11, M=23). Draw a Bohr model showing electron shells.
curriculum.homework: Draw Bohr models for H, C, O, Na, Cl. State protons, neutrons (use approximate mass number = 2 x atomic number), and electrons for each.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: The Periodic Table — Groups and Periods
curriculum.subtopics: Group 1 properties; Group 7 properties; Group 2 properties; Periodic trends
curriculum.lesson_notes: Group 1 (alkali metals): soft, react with water to give hydrogen + alkali, reactivity increases down the group. Group 7 (halogens): coloured, reactive, diatomic molecules, reactivity decreases down the group. Group 2 (alkaline earth metals): less reactive than Group 1. Same group = same outer electrons = similar properties.
curriculum.activity: Group 1 Reaction Observation (Teacher Demonstration): Observe sodium reacting with water. Record observations and explain using particle theory. Safety screen required.
curriculum.homework: Explain why elements in the same group have similar properties. Why does reactivity increase down Group 1 but decrease down Group 7? Use electron shell diagrams to support your answer.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Elements, Compounds, and the Periodic Table — Deeper Understanding
curriculum.subtopics: Metals vs non-metals electronically; Differences in physical properties explained; Introducing ions
curriculum.lesson_notes: Metals: few outer electrons, tend to lose electrons forming positive ions. Non-metals: more outer electrons, tend to gain electrons forming negative ions. This explains conductivity: free electrons in metals. Ions: Na+ (sodium), Cl- (chloride). NaCl is ionic. Relates compound formation to atomic structure.
curriculum.activity: Electrons and Properties Card Game: Cards with electron shell diagrams. Predict metal or non-metal and ion formed based on number of outer electrons.
curriculum.homework: For Mg (Z=12), S (Z=16), K (Z=19): draw electron shell diagram, state number of outer electrons, predict metal or non-metal, predict ion formed.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Chemical Reactions — Word and Symbol Equations
curriculum.subtopics: What happens during a reaction?; Word equations; Balancing symbol equations; Conservation of mass
curriculum.lesson_notes: During chemical reactions, atoms rearrange — none are created or destroyed (Law of Conservation of Mass). Word equations: reactants to products. Symbol equations use chemical formulae. Balancing: add coefficients (not change subscripts) so atoms on each side are equal. Example: 2H2 + O2 to 2H2O.
curriculum.activity: Atom Balancing with Coloured Balls: Use coloured marbles to model a chemical reaction. Physically verify no atoms are lost or gained.
curriculum.homework: Write word equations for: (a) magnesium + oxygen, (b) hydrogen + chlorine, (c) sodium + water. Then balance the symbol equation: Mg + O2 to MgO.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is corrosion?; Rusting — conditions required; Prevention methods; Other corrosion examples
curriculum.lesson_notes: Corrosion: gradual destruction of metal by chemical reaction with environment. Rusting: iron + oxygen + water to iron(III) oxide. Both oxygen AND water are needed. Prevention: painting, oiling, galvanising (zinc coating), stainless steel, electroplating. Zinc is a sacrificial anode. Aluminium forms a protective oxide layer.
curriculum.activity: Rusting Conditions Investigation: Set up 4 iron nail test tubes — open air, dry air with silica gel, water only (boiled and sealed), open air and water. Observe over 1-2 weeks.
curriculum.homework: Explain why iron nails rust outdoors but not in a sealed dry container. Describe two rust prevention methods for a bicycle and explain how each works.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Combustion; Oxidation; Thermal decomposition; Displacement reactions preview
curriculum.lesson_notes: Combustion: substance + oxygen produces heat and light. Complete combustion of hydrocarbons: CO2 + H2O. Incomplete combustion: CO + soot. Oxidation: oxygen added to substance. Thermal decomposition: compound breaks down on heating. Example: copper carbonate (green) to copper oxide (black) + CO2.
curriculum.activity: Thermal Decomposition: Heat copper carbonate in test tube. Observe colour change from green to black. Test gas produced with limewater (turns milky = CO2). Write word equation.
curriculum.homework: Write word equations for: (a) complete combustion of methane, (b) thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate. Write the symbol equation for (a). Explain why CO2 is produced in both.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Test for hydrogen; Test for carbon dioxide; Test for oxygen; Test for water
curriculum.lesson_notes: Test for H2: squeaky pop with burning splint. Test for CO2: bubble through limewater — turns milky. Test for O2: relight a glowing splint. Test for water: anhydrous cobalt chloride paper turns blue to pink; anhydrous copper sulfate turns white to blue. Apply tests to identify products of reactions.
curriculum.activity: Mystery Gas Lab: Three sealed test tubes with unknown gases. Students apply each test, identify each gas, and record results in a table.
curriculum.homework: Design an experiment to confirm burning candle wax produces both water and CO2. Describe method, tests used, and predicted results.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Review: chemical reactions, equations, corrosion; Types of reactions; Chemical tests
curriculum.lesson_notes: Consolidation of all Year 2 Term 1 content. Review: atomic structure, Periodic Table, word and symbol equations, Law of Conservation of Mass, corrosion and prevention, types of reactions, identification tests. Focus on exam technique.
curriculum.activity: GCSE Preview Questions: Checkpoint-style questions under exam conditions (30 minutes). Mark against mark scheme and discuss as peers.
curriculum.homework: Write a revision list for Term 1 Year 2. Include at least 5 key facts for each: atomic structure, Periodic Table groups, types of chemical reaction, and chemical tests.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Exothermic and endothermic reactions; Energy diagrams; Examples; Measuring temperature changes
curriculum.lesson_notes: Exothermic: energy released to surroundings — temperature rises. Examples: combustion, neutralisation, respiration. Endothermic: energy absorbed from surroundings — temperature drops. Examples: thermal decomposition, photosynthesis, dissolving ammonium nitrate. Energy level diagrams show energy of reactants and products.
curriculum.activity: Exo vs Endo Practical: (1) Dissolve calcium chloride in water — temperature rises (exothermic). (2) Dissolve ammonium chloride in water — temperature drops (endothermic). Record changes.
curriculum.homework: Draw energy level diagrams for an exothermic and an endothermic reaction. Label reactants, products, energy released or absorbed. Give one real-life example of each.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Fossil fuels; Environmental impact; Comparing fuels; Introduction to renewable energy alternatives
curriculum.lesson_notes: Fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) are hydrocarbons formed over millions of years. Combustion releases energy. Environmental impacts: CO2 (global warming), SO2 (acid rain), CO (toxic), soot (health). Compare fuels by energy output and environmental impact. Alternatives: solar, wind, hydrogen, biofuels. Renewables are important for sustainability.
curriculum.activity: Fuel Comparison Research: Groups research one fuel type and create a brief poster covering energy output, environmental impact, renewable status, and one use.
curriculum.homework: Explain why burning fossil fuels is an environmental concern. What is acid rain and how is it formed? Why is it difficult to immediately replace fossil fuels with renewables?

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Reactivity series order; Reactions with oxygen; Reactions with water; Reactions with dilute acid
curriculum.lesson_notes: Reactivity series: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Pb > Cu > Ag > Au. With oxygen: reactive metals react vigorously; Al forms protective oxide layer; Au does not react. With water: K explosive, Na vigorous, Ca slowly, Mg very slowly in cold water. With dilute acid: reactive metals produce H2; Cu, Ag, Au do not react.
curriculum.activity: Reactivity Ranking: Test Mg, Zn, Fe, Cu with dilute HCl and water. Observe and record rate of reaction. Place metals in order of reactivity.
curriculum.homework: Using the reactivity series, predict which reactions occur: (a) Mg + zinc sulfate, (b) Cu + iron sulfate, (c) Zn + magnesium sulfate. Write word equations for those that take place.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Metal displacement; Reactivity series and displacement; Competition reactions; Industrial examples
curriculum.lesson_notes: More reactive metal displaces less reactive metal from its salt solution. Example: Zn + CuSO4 to ZnSO4 + Cu. Blue solution turns colourless; reddish copper appears. Displacement only occurs if reacting metal is higher in reactivity series than metal in salt. Thermite reaction: Al + iron oxide is an industrial example.
curriculum.activity: Displacement Reaction: Add zinc to copper sulfate and iron to copper sulfate. Observe colour changes and deposits. Write word equations and explain using the reactivity series.
curriculum.homework: Write word equations for: (a) Mg + CuSO4, (b) Cu + AgNO3, (c) Fe + ZnSO4. For (c), state whether a reaction occurs and explain why using the reactivity series.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is an ore?; Extraction linked to reactivity; Reduction with carbon; Electrolysis for reactive metals
curriculum.lesson_notes: Most metals found as ores (compounds mixed with rock). Extraction method depends on position in reactivity series. Metals below carbon: extracted by reduction with carbon (heated with coke). Metals above carbon: require electrolysis. Gold is found pure because it is unreactive. Iron extracted in a blast furnace.
curriculum.activity: Reduction of Metal Oxides: Heat copper oxide with carbon powder. Observe black oxide turning pink/orange (copper metal). Write word equation: copper oxide + carbon to copper + carbon dioxide.
curriculum.homework: Explain why aluminium requires electrolysis rather than reduction with carbon. Why does this make aluminium more expensive? Describe one environmental issue associated with mining metal ores.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is reaction rate?; Collision theory; Four factors affecting rate: concentration, temperature, surface area, catalyst
curriculum.lesson_notes: Reaction rate: how fast reactants are used up or products form. Collision theory: reactions only occur when particles collide with enough energy (activation energy). Anything increasing collision frequency or energy increases rate. Four factors: concentration, temperature, surface area, and catalysts.
curriculum.activity: Alka-Seltzer Rate Race: Drop tablets in cold, room temperature, and warm water. Time to fully dissolve. Plot results and explain using collision theory.
curriculum.homework: Explain how each factor affects the rate of marble chips + HCl, and why: (a) increasing temperature, (b) grinding marble to powder, (c) increasing acid concentration.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: How concentration affects rate; Measuring reaction rate; Rate graphs; Calculating rate
curriculum.lesson_notes: Higher concentration = more particles per volume = more frequent collisions = faster rate. Rate measured by: timing a colour change, measuring volume of gas produced, or measuring mass change. Rate = change in amount divided by time. Rate graphs: steep initial gradient means fast rate; decreasing gradient as reactants are used up.
curriculum.activity: Sodium Thiosulfate Investigation: React sodium thiosulfate with HCl over a cross. Time for cross to disappear at different concentrations. Plot graph of 1/time vs concentration.
curriculum.homework: 50 cm3 of CO2 was collected in 100 seconds. Calculate the mean rate of reaction in cm3/s. Sketch the volume of gas vs time graph and explain the shape of the curve.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Temperature and Surface Area Effects on Rate
curriculum.subtopics: How temperature affects rate; Activation energy; Surface area effect; Comparison graphs
curriculum.lesson_notes: Temperature: higher temperature means more frequent and more energetic collisions; greater proportion of particles exceed activation energy. Rate approximately doubles for every 10 degree C increase. Surface area: larger surface area means more particles exposed to collide with reactants. Powder reacts faster than lumps. Relevant to industrial catalytic converters and grain dust explosions.
curriculum.activity: Marble Chips vs Powder: React equal masses of marble chips and crushed marble with same HCl. Collect CO2 and compare volumes at 1-minute intervals. Plot both on the same graph.
curriculum.homework: Sketch graphs showing CO2 volume vs time for: (a) marble chips + dilute HCl, (b) marble powder + dilute HCl, (c) marble chips + concentrated HCl. Explain the differences between the curves.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is a catalyst?; How catalysts work; Enzymes; Industrial catalysts
curriculum.lesson_notes: Catalyst: speeds up a reaction without being used up — chemically unchanged at end. Provides alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy. Enzymes are biological catalysts (proteins): amylase breaks down starch; catalase decomposes hydrogen peroxide. Industrial: iron catalyst in Haber Process; platinum in catalytic converters. Catalysts are specific to certain reactions.
curriculum.activity: Hydrogen Peroxide Decomposition: Add H2O2 to (1) water, (2) manganese dioxide powder. Compare rate of oxygen production. Test gas with glowing splint. Weigh MnO2 before and after to confirm it is not consumed.
curriculum.homework: Explain how a catalyst affects: (a) rate of reaction, (b) activation energy, (c) products of the reaction. Give one example of a catalyst used in industry and explain its importance.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Planning an investigation; Controlling variables; Choosing measurement methods; Evaluating results
curriculum.lesson_notes: Planning: identify independent variable (factor tested), dependent variable (what is measured), control variables (kept the same). Choose method for measuring rate. Reliability: repeat measurements, calculate mean, identify anomalies. Evaluating: identify sources of error and suggest improvements.
curriculum.activity: Investigation Design Challenge: Groups design a full investigation to test how temperature affects the rate of sodium thiosulfate + HCl reaction. Write method, results table, and evaluation criteria.
curriculum.homework: Evaluate this investigation: A student tested concentration but accidentally changed the temperature each time. Identify the error and suggest how to improve the investigation.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Review — Reactivity Series and Rates of Reaction
curriculum.subtopics: Reactivity series, displacement, extraction; Reaction rate, collision theory, four factors; Practice questions
curriculum.lesson_notes: Consolidation of all Term 2 content. Review: reactivity series and its uses (predicting displacement and extraction methods), collision theory, four factors affecting rate. Focus on exam technique: drawing and interpreting rate graphs, writing conclusions from data.
curriculum.activity: Speed Round Revision: Rapid-fire questions. Students write answers on mini-whiteboards simultaneously. Review incorrect answers as a class. Categories: reactivity series, displacement, reaction rate.
curriculum.homework: Attempt 10 exam-style questions covering reactivity series and reaction rates (from textbook or teacher-prepared). Self-mark using the answer key.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Full Term 2 review; Exam technique; Application to unfamiliar contexts
curriculum.lesson_notes: Deeper revision and exam readiness for all Year 2 Term 2 content. Practise applying knowledge to unfamiliar scenarios — a key Cambridge Checkpoint skill. Practise interpreting data, drawing conclusions, and evaluating experimental design.
curriculum.activity: Mock Assessment: 40-minute practice paper covering all Term 2 content. Mark against mark scheme in class. Identify areas for improvement.
curriculum.homework: Based on mock assessment results, prepare a targeted revision plan. List the 5 topics where you scored lowest and write 3 key facts for each.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Separating Metals from Ores — Deeper Study
curriculum.subtopics: Blast furnace — iron extraction; Electrolysis — aluminium extraction; Economic and environmental considerations
curriculum.lesson_notes: Blast furnace: iron ore + coke + limestone. Hot air blasted in. Carbon reduces iron oxide to iron. Limestone removes impurities to form slag. Electrolysis of molten aluminium oxide: Al at cathode, O2 at anode. Electrolysis requires large amounts of electricity making aluminium more expensive. Environmental issues: mining damage, CO2 production.
curriculum.activity: Blast Furnace Diagram: Draw and annotate a labelled blast furnace diagram showing reactions at different heights and where iron and slag are removed.
curriculum.homework: Compare iron and aluminium extraction: (a) method used, (b) why different methods are needed (refer to reactivity), (c) which is cheaper and why. Present as a table.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Chemical Reactions in the Earth's Cycles
curriculum.subtopics: The carbon cycle; CO2 and climate; Acid rain from SO2; Limestone reactions
curriculum.lesson_notes: Carbon cycle: CO2 absorbed by photosynthesis; released by respiration, combustion, decomposition. Increasing CO2 from fossil fuels intensifies greenhouse effect. Acid rain: SO2 from burning sulfur-containing fuels + water forms sulfurous acid. Damages limestone buildings, kills trees, acidifies lakes. Limestone heated gives quicklime (CaO); add water gives slaked lime Ca(OH)2.
curriculum.activity: Acid Rain Simulation: Drip dilute acid onto chalk, marble, and granite. Observe bubbling (CO2 released) on chalk and marble. Discuss why limestone buildings are vulnerable.
curriculum.homework: Draw a simplified carbon cycle including photosynthesis, respiration, combustion, decomposition. Explain how burning fossil fuels disturbs the balance of the cycle and possible effects on climate.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: What is electrolysis?; Electrodes and electrolytes; Electrolysis of water; Electrolysis of brine; Products at anode and cathode
curriculum.lesson_notes: Electrolysis: electricity decomposes a compound. Electrolyte: solution or melt containing mobile ions. Positive ions (cations) move to cathode (negative electrode) — gain electrons. Negative ions (anions) move to anode (positive electrode) — lose electrons. Electrolysis of water: H2 at cathode, O2 at anode. Electrolysis of brine: Cl2 at anode, H2 at cathode, NaOH remains.
curriculum.activity: Electrolysis of Water: 9V battery, two pencil electrodes in salt water. Observe gas bubbles at each electrode. Test: glowing splint relights (O2), squeaky pop (H2).
curriculum.homework: In the electrolysis of copper sulfate with carbon electrodes, copper deposits at cathode and oxygen is released at anode. Explain why using ion movement. Write a word equation for the overall reaction.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Electroplating method; Purification of copper; Advantages; Environmental considerations
curriculum.lesson_notes: Electroplating: object to be plated = cathode; plating metal = anode; electrolyte = salt solution of plating metal. Applications: gold or silver plating jewellery, tin-plating steel cans, chrome-plating car bumpers. Copper purification: impure copper anode, pure copper grows at cathode, impurities form sludge. Environmental: energy use and chemical waste.
curriculum.activity: Copper Electroplating: Plate a coin or key with copper using copper sulfate solution and 9V battery. Weigh object before and after. Observe copper coating forming.
curriculum.homework: Describe how to plate a steel spoon with silver: electrolyte, which electrode the spoon would be, what the other electrode would be. Name one industrial application and explain why it is useful.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Stage 9 Review — Rates of Reaction Deeper
curriculum.subtopics: Rates of reaction review; Collision theory and industrial processes; Haber Process and conditions
curriculum.lesson_notes: Haber Process: N2 + 3H2 reversibly produces 2NH3. Conditions: approximately 450 degrees C, approximately 200 atm, iron catalyst. Higher temperature increases rate but decreases yield (exothermic reaction). Higher pressure increases rate and yield. Iron catalyst increases rate without affecting yield — a real-world compromise.
curriculum.activity: Haber Process Analysis: Students plot ammonia yield vs temperature and pressure graphs from provided data. Draw conclusions about optimal industrial conditions.
curriculum.homework: The Haber Process uses 450 degrees C and an iron catalyst. Explain: (a) why a catalyst is used, (b) why 450 degrees C rather than 200 or 600, (c) why high pressure is used, (d) what ammonia is used for.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Organic Chemistry — Introduction to Carbon Compounds
curriculum.subtopics: What is organic chemistry?; Hydrocarbons; Alkanes; Naming and properties
curriculum.lesson_notes: Organic chemistry studies carbon compounds. Carbon forms 4 bonds and can chain together. Hydrocarbons contain only C and H. Alkanes: methane CH4, ethane C2H6, propane C3H8, butane C4H10. Homologous series: each member differs by CH2. Properties: unreactive except combustion. Longer chain = higher boiling point. Crude oil mainly alkanes — separated by fractional distillation.
curriculum.activity: Build Alkane Models: Use molecular model kits or clay balls and sticks to build methane, ethane, propane, butane. Draw structural formula for each.
curriculum.homework: Draw the structural and molecular formula for pentane (5 carbons). Explain why alkanes are a homologous series. Write a word equation for complete combustion of methane.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Polymers and Materials of the Future
curriculum.subtopics: What is a polymer?; Addition polymerisation; Uses of polymers; Environmental issues; Biodegradable materials
curriculum.lesson_notes: Polymers: very large molecules made by joining many monomers together. Addition polymerisation: ethene monomers join to form polyethylene. Properties: flexible, light, waterproof, good insulators. Uses: packaging, clothing (nylon, polyester), medical (PTFE). Environmental concern: non-biodegradable plastics persist in landfill and oceans. Solutions: recycling, biodegradable plastics, reducing single-use plastics.
curriculum.activity: Slime Polymer Making: Mix PVA glue with borax solution to form a cross-linked polymer. Observe and describe properties. Relate to industrial polymer manufacture.
curriculum.homework: Explain what polymerisation means. Why are plastics useful? Give two examples of polymers and their uses. What problems do non-biodegradable plastics cause, and what solutions have been proposed?

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.subtopics: Atomic structure, Periodic Table, reactions; Reactivity series, rates of reaction, electrolysis; Exam technique
curriculum.lesson_notes: Structured revision covering: (1) Atomic structure and Periodic Table, (2) Chemical reactions and equations, (3) Reactivity series and displacement, (4) Rates of reaction and collision theory, (5) Electrolysis. Use past paper questions. Practise calculating rate, predicting displacement, drawing energy diagrams, explaining results.
curriculum.activity: Revision Stations: Five stations around classroom with 2-4 questions each on different themes. Students rotate every 8 minutes. Whole-class review of answers.
curriculum.homework: Complete one full past paper or practice assessment covering all Year 2 topics. Time yourself — allow 45 minutes.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: Application and Synoptic Review — All Years
curriculum.subtopics: Cross-topic review: Year 1 and Year 2; Applying knowledge to real-world contexts; Cambridge Checkpoint style questions
curriculum.lesson_notes: Final synoptic review connecting Year 1 and Year 2 concepts: particle theory to atomic structure; elements to electron structure; changes of state to energy in reactions; rocks and cycles to carbon cycle and industrial chemistry. Apply knowledge to unfamiliar contexts. Interpret data and graphs. Practise Checkpoint-style application questions.
curriculum.activity: Synoptic Challenge Quiz: 15 questions deliberately crossing topics and years. Students attempt individually, discuss in pairs, then whole-class debrief.
curriculum.homework: Write a two-page chemistry portfolio: one concept from each of the 6 terms. Explain clearly and give one real-world application of each.

curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.topic: End of Year 2 Assessment and Course Celebration
curriculum.subtopics: End-of-course assessment; Review and feedback; Progression to Cambridge IGCSE Chemistry
curriculum.lesson_notes: Final end-of-course assessment covering Stages 7, 8, and 9 objectives. Include: short answer, structured experimental, data interpretation, and multi-step reasoning questions. Return assessments with feedback. Discuss how topics link to Cambridge IGCSE Chemistry. Celebrate student achievement.
curriculum.activity: Assessment Day followed by Science Moments sharing: each student shares one thing they found most interesting or surprising in the two-year programme.
curriculum.homework: Reflection Journal: (a) most interesting thing you learned, (b) most challenging topic and how you overcame it, (c) one thing you want to learn more about in IGCSE Chemistry.
